As you can see, these stocks have a 3.21% yield on average, corroborating SCHD’s claim of having an above average yield.

The mere fact of a stock having a high trailing yield doesn’t mean much. If the S&P 500 is going to deliver 10 times the capital gain SCHD’s stocks deliver, the latter stocks having a three times higher yield doesn’t mean much. However, the SCHD stocks have much more going for them than just yield, as I’ll show in the next section.

Valuation

Valuation is a major concern in the markets right now. The S&P 500 currently trades at its second highest P/E ratio since the top in the 2021 growth stock bubble, and its fourth highest P/E ratio ever (see chart below). It also trades at the second highest Shiller P/E ratio of all time. Investors justify these high multiples with the promise of high returns from generative AI. However, these investments are unproven. The big AI companies are still losing money on model development, and nobody even knows whether NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) will keep up its current windfall profitability if the hyperscalers are forced to slow down their AI investments. In an environment like this one, having a value tilt in your portfolio can make a lot of sense.

It’s in this sense that SCHD really shines. SCHD currently has an 18x P/E ratio, a 3.66x P/B ratio, and a 10.38x price to cash flow ratio. The S&P 500, by contrast, trades at 28 times earnings, 5.3 times book, and 38 times cash flow. SCHD certainly looks cheaper. And, its edge in cheapness does not come at the cost of profitability: SCHD’s return on equity [ROE] is a respectable 27%.

So, we can see a clear valuation-based case for investing in SCHD today. The fund trades at lower P/E and price/book ratios than the S&P 500; it has a high return on equity; it has exposure to thriving sectors like energy; and finally, it has a respectable 27% ROE. In light of all these advantages, SCHD’s 3.3% dividend yield is just a bonus.

Operational Fund Characteristics

Some final criteria investors need to look at when analyzing any fund–in addition to its portfolio, management style and theme–are its operational characteristics. These are the characteristics that determine how a fund is built and traded, rather than its investment strategy. Despite their apparently pedestrian nature, these characteristics can have a surprisingly high impact on returns, especially when they are large.

Some of SCHD’s operational characteristics include:

  • A 0.06% total expense ratio. “Total expense ratio” is all of a fund’s expenses, including management fees. 0.06% is lower than average.

  • A 0.03% bid-ask spread. A low bid-ask spread means that market makers can’t capture much of your return when they trade a stock or fund for you. 0.03% is rather low, but not quite as low as the spread on large S&P 500 funds. If you sell a $100,000 block of SCHD stock, market makers will likely pocket about $30. Not too bad.

  • A 0.02% premium to NAV. Funds tend to track their NAV pretty closely. A premium indicates that the price should come down a little; however, 0.02% is such a small premium that it’s probably not something to worry about.

  • 10.4% annualized volatility. This is lower than that of a typical S&P 500 fund.

The above characteristics argue that SCHD is a well structured fund. With its low management fees and bid-ask spread, SCHD is unlikely to send too much of its holders’ money to fund managers or market makers.

The Bottom Line

The Schwab U.S. Equity Dividend ETF is a fund whose time has come. At a time when AI companies are burning cash and trading at 30+ times earnings, SCHD offers a sensibly valued portfolio with plenty of yield to boot. The fund is performing as well as anyone could ask this year, and I expect the outperformance to continue.

data.title: Why The Probability For A Global Recession Is Increasing
data.description:
The Market - Ignoring The Energy Dilemma

Just when it appeared like the market was ready to go for a more significant dip following a challenging first quarter, stocks staged one of their most considerable rallies in recent history.

S&P 500 "SPX" Futures (SP500)

I wrote an article about loading up in early April, and it proved to be a fruitful strategy. However, I must admit, that I did not foresee such an immense market rally, especially in such a short time frame. The S&P 500 has gained about 14% (roughly a typical year's worth of returns) in less than one month.

Nasdaq 100 Futures

The Nasdaq 100's ascent has been even more stellar, as the tech heavy index surged by a staggering 20% during the same timeframe (in less than one month). In retrospect, the rally makes sense, as the market sentiment had become overly depressed, especially in the AI infrastructure related stocks, despite the ongoing Iranian conflict.

Iran Deal Or No Deal

However, despite the enormous upside in stocks recently, the underlying issues with the Iran conflict have not been resolved. On the contrary, Iran still has the highly enriched nuclear material, which it does not appear ready to give up willingly. The U.S. now has three aircraft carriers in theater, in addition to the blockade, and oil prices continue hovering around $100.

There is considerable risk for kinetic hostilities to resume, in my view, and the disruptions in energy markets are increasing the probability of a global recession. While the U.S. is somewhat insulated, it is not immune, and a global energy shock will likely reverberate around the globe, hitting the U.S. hard as well. Due to this dynamic, I've become more cautious on markets in the near term, as well as longer term. In addition to the uncertainty surrounding energy markets, which could eventually lead to demand destruction, there are other serious issues to consider.

AI - Taking Over Jobs

The AI takeover has already started. Many big tech companies are announcing enormous job cuts in order to justify their gargantuan AI spending, and also presumably because of AI optimization and automation. Mass layoffs could lead to the erosion in consumer sentiment and could impact consumer demand very negatively. While we may all benefit from AI innovation and automation in the future, the transition process could be very messy, and it may impact many people and markets negatively as we advance.

Jobs Market Likely to Worsen

The U.S. jobs market has already illustrated signs of softening. While we have seen some uneven numbers in the recent data, the jobs image could become worse as more companies cut jobs to increase efficiency. This dynamic could be extremely detrimental to parts of corporate America, and markets in general, as consumer spending worsens. Let's not forget that the U.S. economy is largely consumer driven, as consumer spending accounts for roughly 68% of U.S. GDP.

Consumer Spending Likely To Worsen

Consumer sentiment recently hit its lowest level on record. This dynamic is very atypical, as we typically see ultra-low consumer sentiment figures when we approach a low point in a bear market. However, to see consumer sentiment this awful when the market is surging to new ATHs is a problematic scenario, in my view.

This dynamic implies that while Wall Street is making money hand over fist, Main Street is struggling, and those struggles could worsen. Therefore, we have a divergence, a tale of two economies if you will. However, this divergence can't last forever, and in time, the market could revert to the mean, meaning considerable downside for the general market.

Additionally, sky-high mortgage, near record credit card, and other high rates are another negative headwind for an already exhausted consumer economy. Add increasing gas, fuel, and all other costs that higher oil prices impact, and you may have just the recipe to tip the U.S. economy over into a recession.

Monetary Policy Uncertainty

As the new Fed Chair was nominated, I wrote that the appointment of Kevin Warsh was a policy error, and I still believe that it was. Warsh is moving through the confirmation process, but he has been vocal about reducing the Fed's $6.3 trillion balance sheet, and was not in favor of QE when previously serving on the FOMC.

In other words, there is little probability for QE, especially as it concerns longer term treasuries, which bring down mortgage rates. We may even see quantitative tightening, as the Fed looks to unload its balance sheet, as Warsh implied he would prefer. As far as decreasing interest rates. This dynamic will be highly challenging given the protracted Iran conflict, and the enormous impact on global oil and gas supply and demand. Inflation could continue rising, and it's nowhere near the Fed's 2% target rate. In fact, inflation has been moving in the opposite direction, which complicates things greatly for the Fed and the market, in general.

Rate Probabilities For 2026

There is less than a 25% chance that we will see a rate cut before the end of the year. This dynamic may not change even as Kevin Warsh takes over as the new Chief at the Fed. Despite the jobs market being lackluster, which could potentially worsen due to AI and other headwinds, there is little wiggle room for the Fed to intervene because of the elevated inflation. Which brings us to the real bright spot in the economy, which is of course AI.

What Happens When The AI Buildout Slows?

We've been in the AI expansion cycle for some time now. We've watched stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) go from about $10 (split adjusted) in late 2022 to over $200 recently. We've seen Palantir (PLTR) go from $6 to over $200. The gains in the Mag stocks and the AI infrastructure segment have been amazing.

However, my number one question is what will happen when the inevitable slowdown comes? Hyperscalers can't spend hundreds of billions of dollars annually on AI infrastructure indefinitely, and there will come a point when the "music stops." The question is not if the music will end, but when, and there could be many people left standing, holding the preverbal bag in the end.

Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Apple (AAPL), are all reporting earnings in the upcoming sessions, and it's going to be very interesting what kind of AI spending plans these companies have left. There is also the debate whether there is too much spending, and whether it will pay off or not eventually.

Circular Financing Deals

We see many circular financing deals occurring, and they will likely continue to occur. However, I think that this dynamic is quite troubling. Circular financing is very interesting. Here is how it works. For instance, a company like Nvidia invests ten or thirty billion in a company like OpenAI.

OpenAI then goes back to Nvidia, and purchases tens of billions worth of GPUs from Nvidia. Also, a company like Oracle (ORCL) can get tens of billions in loans to build out data centers for potential future revenues from OpenAI and others. Again, going to Nvidia to purchase GPUs for the data centers.

What about if OpenAI won't be able to pay its future obligations for whatever reason? How would Oracle pay back its debt then? Who will Nvidia sell its GPUs to when the system simply has too much supply, and increased competition? Margins could drop, growth and profitability could decline.

Not many people seem to be concerned with this potential dynamic during this AI frenzy. However, if such issues arise, they could have significant systemic implications for the AI industry, the banks and other institutions involved with the circular financing, and the financial system, in general. Therefore, concerns persist, and I am more cautious on the AI segment here, as I believe we may be in the later stages of the AI infrastructure buildout cycle now.

One More Chart For The Road Ahead

Just take a look at the semiconductor ETF (SOXX). It's up by nearly 170% over the last year, but this latest move has been "extraordinary." The SOXX surged by about 50% just in the last month alone. Semiconductor stocks are surging like the AI boom will last forever. However, I am concerned that the market may be getting ahead of itself. The AI bubble will not inflate indefinitely, and with price action like this, I am becoming more cautious. I've sold a lot of semiconductor holdings recently, including even releasing my Marvell (MRVL), AMD (AMD), and Nvidia (NVDA) stakes. Now, while I still may get back in on pullbacks, I'm certainly not chasing the semis here now.

Moreover, I think a considerable pullback in this space and the broader market could occur, as not all is well with the economy. Furthermore, in the intermediate term, there is increased risk of a recession, and a potential market crash to rival the best of them longer term (next 3-5 years). We've done phenomenally well in recent years, especially during the AI run up with stocks like Palantir, Nvidia, Meta, Alphabet, Amazon, and many others. However, I don't think that now is the time to remain uber bullish on these and other high profile AI names.

Instead, I prefer to protect my gains, and take a more practical and a more defensive approach now, even if there is marginal upside ahead. The risks are starting to outweigh the potential rewards, in my view. Having said that, we may still have a solid year in 2026, but due to the increased uncertainty I am widening my 2026 year-end target range to 7,200-7,700.

data.title: AMD: Strong AI Tailwinds, But Valuation Is Getting Ahead Of Reality
data.description:
Overview

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) has emerged as one of the most important beneficiaries of the AI infrastructure boom, with its rapid expansion in data centers and AI accelerators driving a sharp re-rating in its stock price.

AMD is a fabless semiconductor company which designs high-performance chips powering everything from cloud data centers to personal computers and gaming systems. Over the past decade, AMD has transformed itself from a struggling CPU vendor into a full-stack compute platform company and is today set to compete against giants like Nvidia (NVDA). The stock’s strong performance has been driven by expectations of sustained growth in AI infrastructure spending and AMD’s increasing participation in data center workloads.

AMD operates a fabless model and it designs chips internally and outsources the fabrication to foundries, primarily TSMC (TSM). This model allows AMD to avoid heavy capex, focus on design innovation and scale easily. Having said that, the need for foundries creates supply chain dependence and limited control over production timelines which is risky given the high pace of technological growth and therefore, high time-to-market could mean lost revenue.

AMD’s key strength lies in the broad and integrated compute portfolio and reports three business segments – Data Center; Client & Gaming; Embedded. AMD is one of the players which is positioning itself at the forefront of the AI race and stands to gain significantly from the increased AI adoption. However, what remains to be seen is whether the high valuation of AMD is justified and whether there are hidden risks that investors may be potentially missing out on that could be a red flag for the business model and subsequent performance of the shares.

However, what remains to be seen is whether AMD can translate its current momentum into sustained market share gains in AI accelerators and GPUs over players like Nvidia and Intel (INTC) and whether now might be the best time investors to cash in on this optimism, or whether investors are currently being overly optimistic in affording a premium valuation to AMD, leading to inflated valuation.

Tailwinds

Data-Center and GPU Led Growth

AMD’s data-center led business has become its primary revenue source, recording $16.6 billion revenue in FY25, representing a 32% YoY growth and $5.4 billion revenue in Q4, FY25, representing a 39% YoY growth. The data center segment now contributes approximately 48% of the total revenue. These figures highlight the transition towards the data-center led model which is structurally more durable and higher margin than the legacy PC business, thereby improving earnings prospects in the future.

One of the primary drivers of growth for AMD is the EPYC processor. The reason for the popularity of the EPYC processor is given below as a comparison with two-socket Intel server, with two Intel Xeon Gold 6262V processors to a one-socket server with one AMD EPYC 7702P processor.

The figure above shows that the EPYC processor outperforms the two Intel Xeon Gold 6262V processors in every parameter. This translates into lower cost of ownership for the customers of AMD, which is a critical purchasing criterion for hyperscalers and enterprises, thereby enabling AMD to structurally gain market share from Intel. This has led to record CPU sales, as mentioned in the earnings call. AMD further mentioned that there has been strong adoption across cloud and enterprise with hyperscalers launching 500+ AMD-based instances.

With the rise of data centers and AI adoption, the need for parallel processing has increased. This is where GPUs become more important as they are better at parallel processing vis-a-vis CPUs which are better at sequential processing. Moreover, GPUs also hold higher memory bandwidth, having to do high-calculation oriented tasks.

AMD today has designed GPUs which are specifically for AI training, AI inference and so on with 8 of the top 10 AI companies using them. I am bullish on the GPU specifically because of the tremendous market potential that this holds as the graph below shows, with exponential growth expected in the coming years. AMD’s Instinct product portfolio stands to benefit from this rapid expansion of AI infrastructure spending.

This has been driven largely by increased adoption of AI and especially agentic AI. The graph below shows the relative composition of CPU and GPU for traditional LLM on one hand and agentic AI on the other hand.

A key benefit of this is that AMD earns higher margins on GPU sales vis-a-vis CPU sales. Therefore, rise in demand for GPU implies higher profit margins for AMD which is likely to translate to higher valuation.

AMD is operating in the AI accelerator space. AI accelerators are designed in hardware such that they perform certain AI-related operations like matrix multiplication, vector operations extremely fast. AMD is releasing the AMD Instinct MI400 series, which is expected to further strengthen its positioning in AI accelerators, although its competitiveness relative to Nvidia’s next-generation architectures will depend on both hardware performance and the continued development of its software ecosystem launching in 2026. It is a next-generation AI accelerator designed for training trillion-parameter models and competing with Nvidia's Rubin architecture. The upcoming MI400 is expected to have significantly enhanced performance compared to the current MI350 series.

AMD is positioning the MI400 lineup to Nvidia's upcoming Vera Rubin series, which claims similar compute performance and memory bandwidth, but 1.5x higher memory capacity and scale-out bandwidth.

Transition from Vendor to Infrastructure Partner

A key transition that AMD is making is the shift from being a seller of chip designs to becoming an infrastructure partner. This has been with a focus towards providing co-design service to hyperscalers. Hyperscalers now aim to optimize entire systems which involve hardware, software and workloads. Therefore, AMD's role is now to customize GPUs for specific workloads, optimizing memory, bandwidth and architecture and also ensuring compatibility with software stacks.

A prime advantage of this model is that the switching costs are high. If a hyperscaler for example wants to change from AMD to Nvidia, then it will need to rewrite software, retrain models on new hardware, redesign data center architecture and so on. The stickiness of revenue also affords revenue visibility and contract duration to AMD, although pricing remains influenced by hyperscaler bargaining power, allowing it to earn higher margins per contract and also for a more prolonged period due to the contractual nature of the revenue.

One such primary example is the recent partnership deal that AMD has signed with Meta (META). The agreement states a multi-year strategic partnership to power Meta AI’s infrastructure using AMD’s advanced computing technology. It involves a 6-gigawatt agreement for AI infrastructure deployment.

AMD has also signed a 6 gigawatt agreement for AMD’s Instinct GPUs which will power OpenAI’s AI infrastructure to ensure large-scale AI deployment. This will not only enhance the earnings of AMD, but also strengthen its position in the high-performance computing and AI markets.

Headwinds

Nvidia Dominance & Software Ecosystem Moat

As previously discussed, while AMD has made significant progress in AI GPUs, what is worth noting is that the primary challenge is not just competing on hardware, but AMD also needs to compete against Nvidia’s deeply entrenched software ecosystem. This is one of the reasons it will prove difficult to adopt AMD GPUs given the resulting stickiness.

Therefore, a critical challenge for AMD lies in the fact that competitive advantage in AI infrastructure is driven less by hardware performance and more by software ecosystem strength. While AMD continues to improve its GPU capabilities, AI development workflows remain deeply integrated with Nvidia’s CUDA platform, which serves as the de facto standard for training and deploying machine learning models. As a result, switching costs extend beyond hardware replacement to include rewriting code, re-optimizing models, and retraining engineering teams.

AMD has launched ROCm to counter this disadvantage. This has witnessed significant improvement as it now supports LLMs. However, while the quality has improved, it remains small in the developer ecosystem and fewer optimized libraries. Therefore, what remains to be seen is how ROCm performs in the coming times. If it is able to catch up with Nvidia’s CUDA, AMD can compete on the same footing as Nvidia. As a result, even with competitive hardware, AMD’s ability to gain meaningful share may be constrained unless it can significantly narrow the software ecosystem gap, making ROCm development a critical factor for long-term competitiveness.

Revenue Concentration & Geopolitical Risks

Among the trend of hyperscalers and infrastructure partner revenue tie-ups, an emerging risk in this is that of revenue concentration among a few customers of AMD. As previously mentioned AMD has got into partnerships with some of the major hyperscalers like Meta. While the dependence on these hyperscalers leads to a stable source of revenue for a more prolonged period, it also creates dependency on these players for revenue which can be a cause for concern because hyperscalers are likely to have high bargaining power due to their sheer size, offering negotiation leverage.

In addition to this, there is also geopolitical risk to be considered. One of the key concerns in this aspect is that AMD cannot freely sell its chips to China due to national security concerns as the U.S. government has restricted the export of high-performance GPUs and AI accelerators. This effectively cuts off AMD from a major market that is currently available. Naturally there is also the fear of retaliation from China which could block the sales of rare-earth metals which are crucial in the semiconductor value chain.

Financials & Valuation

To understand AMD’s value to investors, it’s important to understand the past financial performance. The graph below shows the revenue and profit margins trend.

It is clear that revenue has increased rapidly and almost consistently every year while profit margins rose initially to an all-time high in 2020-21, but declined post that. The rise in profit margins was driven by a mix of factors like covid-time demand, traction gained by Ryzen in the PC business. The decline in profit margins were driven by a mix of factors like integration costs pertaining to the acquisition of Xilinx, PC market slowdown and cost pressures. The recent recovery in profit margins has been driven by AI-market demand growth. What I believe is that if AMD were to continue making the right strategic decisions pertaining to the markets, the profit margins recovery would be a permanent rather than a temporary phenomena. Going forward, I believe that there is likely to be sustained margin expansion stability as the contribution from data centers increase and the dependence on PC segment and gaming reduces.

To understand what are the primary revenue drivers for AMD, we need to get a closer look at the revenue segments. The graph below shows the breakdown.

In the past three years, data centers have gained rapidly to account for an increasing portion of the revenue and the importance of other segments like client (driven by Ryzen) and gaming (driven by AMD Radeon) have both reduced. This backs my claim about the importance of data centers and how AMD is taking steps to position itself at the forefront in this rapidly evolving market. The increasing contribution from the data center segment indicates a structural shift toward higher-margin and more resilient revenue streams. This trend highlights a structural shift toward higher-margin data center revenues, although the volatility in margins underscores AMD’s continued exposure to semiconductor cyclicality.

However, what needs to be understood is whether investors are currently unaware about the factors and are yet to cash in on this opportunity or are currently being overly optimistic. Therefore I performed a valuation exercise to gain an understanding of the same. The figure below provides a comparison of AMD with the other players over some of the key ratios.

The picture is clear that AMD is currently valued at a significant premium over the other players. Moreover, AMD is also trading at a premium compared to its 5-year average. However, what needs to be seen yet is how AMD is valued vis-a-vis Nvidia, one of its primary competitors which is currently outperforming AMD in the GPU space. The figure below highlights the valuation comparison.

An unexpected trend is that AMD is trading at a premium compared to Nvidia across most ratios. This implies that AMD may be overvalued compared to its performance levels because even after improvements, AMD faces heavy competition from Nvidia due to the CUDA dominance which partially makes the premium valuation unjustified for AMD.

Conclusion

AMD currently presents a compelling long-term growth story, driven by its focus on data centers and AI accelerator markets, along with an improving revenue mix and model. The company’s improvement in Instinct GPU portfolio, as well as deepening partnership with hyperscalers highlights the potential to capitalize on the expanding AI infrastructure opportunity.

However, there are significant hurdles that AMD continues to face. The dominance of Nvidia in the AI accelerator market is primarily driven by the dominance in the entrenched software ecosystem driven by CUDA which acts as a structural barrier for AMD. Apart from this there are also risks of customer concentration and geopolitical risks. However, what remains to be seen is whether AMD is able to position its ROCm well enough to compete with Nvidia’s CUDA.

The valuation exercise provides support that AMD may be overvalued due to excess optimism of the investors. While I understand the sentiment associated with data center growth and infrastructure partnership, the high valuation accorded to AMD is not entirely justified currently.

Having considered all the factors, AMD presents a balanced risk-reward profile, where sustained upside will depend on successful execution in scaling its AI GPU business and narrowing the ecosystem gap with industry leaders. Therefore, my stance will be cautious for AMD and I will wait for a price dip before cashing in on AMD as the high valuation is currently not justified unless AMD makes a strong breakthrough in the ROCm space specially. So, my recommendation would be a Hold.

data.title: Verizon: Fiber Growth Engine To Accelerate
data.description:
Verizon Communications Inc.'s (VZ) results for Q1 ’26 signaled a strong start to the year: the telecommunications provider reported $1.28 per share in non-GAAP earnings, surpassing the analyst consensus of $1.21 per share. Verizon’s total revenues reached $34.4B but fell slightly short of the consensus prediction by $447M.

Verizon completed its $20.0B Frontier acquisition in the first quarter, which is set to add considerable cost synergy potential to the telecommunications platform. The acquisition is also set to expand the company’s fiber broadband footprint, which is already a major growth engine for Verizon.

The free cash flow outlook for FY 2026 implies solid growth as well, on the back of the Frontier acquisition, and with an estimated forward dividend coverage ratio of 180%, Verizon is set to increase its dividend for another year. Shares are too cheap to make sense, and I see a favorable risk profile for income investors specifically.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Verizon a Strong Buy in July 2025 --Verizon: Raised Guidance, 1.75X Coverage, Strong Yield -- amid growth in its core broadband business. Verizon is also growing its dividend, which distinguishes the telecom from its most formidable competitor in the industry, AT&T (T). With the Frontier acquisition completed in Q1 '26, Verizon is set to accelerate its growth in the critical broadband segment, which is backing up strong free cash flow projections. The dividend is well supported by cash flow and will continue to grow in 2026, potentially at a faster rate than in the telecom's past.

Broadband Growth is Set to Accelerate in 2026

Verizon closed its $20.0B acquisition of Frontier Communications on January 20, 2026, which added 2.2M fiber subscribers and expanded Verizon's footprint into previously untapped regions such as Texas, California, and West Virginia. The expanded footprint now makes Verizon's fiber broadband services available to 30 million homes and businesses in 31 states plus Washington, D.C.

With the Frontier acquisition, Verizon is not only expanding its broadband network, but it is also creating a vital catalyst for its most important business from a growth perspective: the telecom seeks to expand its network to 40-50M by the end of the decade, assuming an additional addition of at least 2M fiber passings.

On an organic basis, Verizon had 341,000 broadband net adds in Q1'26, which lifted the firm’s total fiber broadband connections to 16.8M, reflecting a massive jump of 29% compared to the year-earlier period. This growth was made up of 2.2M fiber subscribers who moved over from Frontier plus organic growth in Verizon's Fixed Wireless Access business.

While the growth was not as strong as it was in the year-earlier period -- Verizon added 353,000 new broadband customers in Q1'25 on a net basis -- the company is clearly benefiting from positive subscriber momentum that is tied to the firm's massive fiber broadband build-out. As the firm adds more fiber connections and more deeply services its new territories, Verizon has a strong outlook for accelerating subscriber growth in its core business in FY 2026.

Free Cash Flow and Forward Dividend Coverage Ratio

Strong broadband performance lifted Verizon’s free cash flow to $3.8B in the first fiscal quarter, showing 6% year-over-year growth. According to Verizon’s Q1 ’26 supplemental report on Monday, the telecommunications platform now expects to generate $21.5B in free cash in FY 2026, which implies 7% year-over-year growth.

This projected free cash flow growth is related to Verizon expecting strong growth in broadband subscribers as well as annual run-rate cost synergies in the amount of $1.0B (to be fully realized by FY 2028) related to the Frontier acquisition. Verizon also sees ~5-6% adjusted EPS growth this year (previously 4-5%), which may positively impact the telecom's dividend growth rate later this year.

The goal to achieve $1.0B in operating expense savings from the Frontier acquisition comes on top of a $5.0B transformation program announced by the company that seeks to utilize AI, layoffs (Verizon already cut 13,000 positions at the end of 2025), and real estate optimizations to maximize the telecom's long-term free cash flow potential.

The outlook for FY 2026 allows me to calculate the estimated forward dividend coverage ratio for Verizon: assuming that the telecom will increase its dividend ~2% in September 2026 (the firm's historical dividend growth rate), the company is on track to pay out a total of $2.86 per share for the current year.

Since the telecommunications provider has 4.22B shares outstanding -- the Frontier acquisition was an all-cash deal and didn’t result in a higher number of issued shares -- Verizon could post a forward dividend coverage ratio of 180% (the calculation implies a total dividend payout of $11.9B).

In AT&T's case, which I just covered with a Strong Buy rating, the telecommunications provider has an estimated dividend coverage ratio of 200% for the current fiscal year. In both AT&T's and Verizon's cases, the dividend should be very safe in 2026, as both telecoms generate more than sufficient free cash flow to pay their dividend obligations while retaining cash for ambitious capital expenditure programs. Verizon, as an example, confirmed that it plans to spend $16.0-16.5B in CapEx in FY 2026 amid a broad-scale acceleration of the telecom's fiber build-out.

Verizon’s Low P/E Creates Upside for Investors in Addition to a 9% Yield

The telecommunications company is valued at just 9.0X next year’s projected consensus earnings -- which stand at $5.24 per-share -- while AT&T is priced at a forward P/E ratio of 10.0X.

In my AT&T work, covering the telecom's first-quarter earnings, I highlighted that strong broadband growth prospects could lead to a higher earnings-based valuation factor, not only for AT&T but also for Verizon.

Since Verizon has stronger free cash flow than its telecom rival -- AT&T is guiding for about $18B in FCF in the current fiscal year -- and has a lower P/E ratio, I see a slightly better valuation and risk profile for Verizon. Plus, as mentioned earlier, Verizon is growing its dividend while AT&T is not.

An 11-12X P/E ratio for Verizon, which in my opinion is achievable on the back of growing broadband subscriber momentum and a larger fiber broadband footprint, implies a fair value range of $58-63 per share, highlighting up to 34% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with Verizon

The main risk for Verizon following the acquisition of Frontier’s fiber operations is that the telecom may not realize its targeted goal of $1.0B in run-rate cost synergies, which may negatively impact the company’s free cash flow guidance for 2026. What could get me to change my views about Verizon is if the telecom were to see slow integration success of Frontier's fiber broadband operations. Also, if Verizon fails to accelerate its growth in broadband subscriber net-add growth throughout the year, I would lean towards downgrading shares of the telecom to Hold.

Final Thoughts

Verizon is set to seriously grow in the fiber business in 2026 after the integration of Frontier’s fiber locations into its broadband network. This absorption creates significant subscriber growth surprise potential and sets the telecom up for an acceleration of its growth in its most promising business: broadband.

If Verizon can additionally capture $1.0B in long-term run-rate cost synergies related to the Frontier acquisition and continue to raise its dividend, potentially even at a higher rate than 2% given its strong adjusted EPS outlook, the telecom could potentially be an outperformance candidate in 2026. With shares being cheap, trading at 9.0X forward earnings, the risk profile for Verizon is extraordinarily skewed to the upside, with the telecom having up to 34% upside potential to the top end of my fair value range.

data.title: Iovance Biotherapeutics: Buy, Because Short-Term Competitor Is Out
data.description:
The recent collapse of Replimune’s lead program has, in the most definitive sense, cleared the competitive runway for Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA) in the short term, reinforcing its dominant position in the post-PD-1 melanoma market. Indeed, as of April 10, 2026, the FDA issued a second and final Complete Response Letter (CRL) to Replimune for its oncolytic virus, RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec), in combination with nivolumab.

In this article, I will deep dive into Iovance's fundamentals and recent clinical progress to reach a conclusion that now that Replimune is out of the picture, Iovance is a BUY for its rapid ramp in melanoma as well as potential expansion into larger indications such as mNSCLC.

Company overview

Iovance Biotherapeutics is a pioneer in the field of polyclonal tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapies, a form of personalized regenerative medicine that leverages a patient's own immune system to target diverse neoantigens in solid tumors. The company achieved a landmark milestone with the 2024 accelerated approval of Amtagvi (lifileucel), the first-ever T-cell therapy for a solid tumor indication. Iovance operates its own centralized manufacturing facility, the Iovance Cell Therapy Center (iCTC), which is critical for the complex 22-to-34-day expansion process required to produce individualized doses from surgically removed tumor tissue. Note that TIL therapy requires a different and significantly more challenging manufacturing process compared to the more well-established autologous CAR-T, resulting in higher failure rate and lower gross margin.

Amtagvi (lifileucel) and Pipeline

The clinical pipeline is anchored by Amtagvi (lifileucel), currently FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma and in Phase 3 (TILVANCE-301) for frontline advanced melanoma. Secondary lead lifileucel is in registrational trials for second-line advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Recent real-world data for lifileucel in post-PD-1 melanoma demonstrated a confirmed objective response rate (ORR) of 44%, surpassing the 31% ORR from the pivotal C-144-01 trial that supported its accelerated approval. All presented data in the article can be found here.

Crucially, the 52% ORR observed in earlier lines of therapy suggests a best-in-class profile if moved into frontline settings. This is not surprising given patients with fewer prior treatments have better/stronger immune systems and respond better to immunotherapies such as CAR-T and TIL. Of note, however, the requirement for high-dose IL-2 (aldesleukin) post-infusion remains a significant safety hurdle, as it causes severe chills, fever, and transient hypotension, requiring inpatient monitoring.

Efficacy comparisons against the standard of care for second-line NSCLC—typically docetaxel—show a potential superior profile for lifileucel . Early data reported a 26% ORR and a median duration of response (mDOR) exceeding 25 months, doubling the historical 12.8% ORR seen with docetaxel. Frontline NSCLC cohort is even better, with 64.3% ORR for EGFR-WT subgroup, and 54.5% ORR for EGFR-WT PD-L1 negative subgroup, supporting moving lifileucel to frontline NSCLC will likely achieve a better response rate, which again echoes the same observation in advanced melanoma, where earlier adoption of lifileucel correlated with better ORR.

While these results are promising, the "living drug" nature of TILs makes them far more complex than "me-too" small molecules or mAbs, positioning Iovance as a unique but operationally burdened player in the oncology space.

Upcoming catalysts in the pipeline

The most critical catalyst for 2026 is the initiation of Phase 2 registrational trials for LN-145 in advanced undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) and dedifferentiated liposarcoma (DDLPS), following a 50% ORR in early cohorts. Additionally, an Investigational New Drug (IND) submission for IOV-5001, a next-generation genetically engineered TIL therapy, is also expected in the first half of 2026. This next-gen TIL therapy is designed to work without the need for follow-on high-dose interleukin-2 (IL-2)

Longer term the market is awaiting mature data from the TILVANCE-301 trial in frontline melanoma, which is necessary to convert lifileucel's accelerated approval into full approval.

Based on historical benchmarks for cell therapies in solid tumors, the Probability of Success (PoS) for full approval in melanoma is high (roughly 75-80%) given the durability of the five-year data already presented. However, the PoS for NSCLC remains more conservative (45-50%) due to the biological complexity of lung tumors and the competitive intensity of the indication. The primary risk to these catalysts is not just efficacy, but the CMC hurdles inherent in scaling personalized cell therapy.

Competitive Landscape

Iovance faces competition from both established checkpoint inhibitors and emerging cellular therapies. In the melanoma space, the landscape cleared quite a lot after the FDA rejected Replimune’s RP1 in early 2026, removing a head-on near-term rival. However, big pharma maintains dominance in the earlier lines of therapy that Iovance hopes to penetrate.

Competitor Drug Name Indication Status Annual Sales (Est. 2025)
Merck Keytruda Melanoma / NSCLC Approved $28B+
BMS Opdivo Melanoma / NSCLC Approved $9B+
Sanofi Proleukin (IL-2) Adjunct Therapy Approved ~$45M (to IOVA)
Obsidian Bio OBX-115 Melanoma Phase 1/2 N/A
Achilles Tx ATL001 NSCLC / Melanoma Phase 1/2 N/A

Financial Health

According to the Q4 2025 report and subsequent April 2026 financial updates, Iovance maintains a substantial liquidity cushion. As of December 31, 2025, the company held approximately $303 million in cash. The quarterly burn rate is currently estimated at $75–$80 million, driven by the expansion of authorized treatment centers (ATCs) and the high costs of Phase 3 clinical trials. Based on this burn rate, the company has a runway of approximately 3-4 quarters, extending into Q3 2027. This presents some dilution risks before the company can turn a profit from lifileucel.

Valuation

For the commercialized melanoma indication, lifileucel generated approximately $220 million in its first full year (2025). Peak sales for the melanoma indication are projected at $1.2 billion by 2030, given the current $6 billion TAM for metastatic melanoma. Using a median P/S ratio of 3, the melanoma business is valued at approximately $3.6 billion.

For the pre-commercial pipeline, particularly lifileucel in NSCLC, predicted peak sales are estimated at $1.5 billion. Applying a risk-adjusted PoS of 45%, the risk-adjusted peak sales value is $675 million. Using the P/S ratio of 3, the pipeline adds an estimated $2.025 billion in valuation. Combining the commercial melanoma value ($3.6B) with the risk-adjusted pipeline value ($2.025B) yields a total enterprise valuation of approximately $5.625 billion. At a current enterprise valuation of roughly $1.17 billion, the company appears fundamentally undervalued if it can successfully execute its commercial ramp and clinical trials.

Risks to the company

The primary risk to Iovance is the successful commercial ramp of lifileucel, which suffers from the operational bottleneck and high cost of "living drugs." TIL therapy requires an arduous process of tumor biopsy, centralized shipping, and a 34-day expansion window where any manufacturing failure results in a total loss of the dose—potentially a death sentence for patients with rapidly progressing disease. Furthermore, the $515,000 price tag and the requirement for inpatient monitoring limit adoption to specialized academic centers, creating a "ceiling" on market penetration.

Regulatory risk remains persistent; the FDA recently issued an untitled letter regarding "misleading" survival claims in promotional materials, signaling that Iovance remains under heavy scrutiny as it attempts to move lifileucel into earlier treatment lines.

Lastly is the C-suit management team, and this is an interesting one. The former CEO of IOVA, Maria Fardis, Ph.D., resigned as the President, CEO, and a member of the Board of Directors of Iovance Biotherapeutics on May 18, 2021. Officially the reason was to pursue other opportunities, but her resignation came immediately after the company announced a delay in its Biologics License Application (BLA) for its lead cancer therapy, lifileucel, due to FDA questions regarding potency assays.

Since then, Frederick Vogt, Ph.D.—the company's former General Counsel—was appointed interim President and CEO and has served in that capacity for five years. There is apparently no plan to search for a permanent CEO anytime soon. While it is difficult to determine if a General Counsel can make a successful CEO given their different skill sets, this current interim CEO has poor reviews on Glassdoor. Although I usually don't place much weight on Glassdoor ratings, this level of negativity is rare, even in fast-paced biotech companies where burnout is common. This leadership risk is worth monitoring if you are invested for the long haul.

Final Verdict

As of late April 2026, Iovance Biotherapeutics represents a case of successful clinical de-risking balanced against significant commercial execution pressure. While the regulatory path is clear, the market now requires proof that the company can transform 'living drugs' into a profitable, scalable business. In my view, Iovance is successfully ramping up the commercialization of lifileucel, evidenced by its improving gross margins, which have now reached 50%. Consequently, I rate the stock a BUY.
